speaker: interviewer
text: We're on take six with Mrs. Rachel Warszauer Schlufman, August 29th, 1995. You were telling me about the Skarzysko camp and how it was divided into three sections, A, B, and C.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which section were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in section A, which was the best. The work was not bad, decent. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have to do? What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, we had to work ammunition bullets for the-- ammunition work, big machines. We work, you know, from seven in the morning to six in the evening. We had day shifts, night shifts. You know, every time we, you know, we switched-- not we. But they made us switch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the food-- what can I tell you? My first experience with the food-- I had-- when I came into the ghet-- came into the camp, I was, I was like, I was shock. I was like in a shock seeing these people like skeletons. Such a skeletons when you see it on the picture now. For me it was something weird to see it, [? no, ?] with the stomachs out, bulging out, with the ears, with the eyes. Their legs so skinny, I couldn't believe it. Is this what-- will I look like them? Is everybody going to-- but other ones looked better.

speaker: interviewee
text: That were the ones who brought them from Warsaw ghetto, brought them from other camps. They were wear out already. They, they worked already for two years. I came in 1942. These worked already in other camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were without-- they were gone. With the last breath, they worked. They kept them. I don't know why they brought them to, to Skarzysko. But they kept them for a few weeks. And then they sent them with evacuation, to send them to Treblinka, to Auschwitz, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: The men looked worse than the women. Seeing the men, they looked terrible-- skeletons, [? Muslims, ?] terrible. The women somehow didn't look that bad. And well, you stood in the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep? Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us a bunk with 20 girls. I took the top, because for cleanliness, I wanted the top floor-- Because it was shaking the mud with the shaking from the straw, you know, little straw, muddy straw. Each, each person, you know, when they sleep, they-- it was splits, you know. So the straw fell down on the faces. So I-- I chose the top floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was hard to get up, you know? But I chose it for that, because I was afraid. They told me-- when I came into the bunk, there were girls already. And she says, the girl on the bottom says, well, I cannot climb on top. Because she says, if you can do it, you take the top. It will be cleaner. You-- I says, I do it. I don't mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a terrible thing with the appells in the camp. We stood for two days without working, just to get accustomed to that camp, walking around in that, in the places, seeing these men, seeing the women. The women were standing in a different part. You know, it was separated, the women barracks. And the men's barrack were on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: A father used to come to visit his daughter-- or a young father, of course. They weren't old people. The ages were young. The oldest could be like 36, 35-- 38 maybe one or two, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was waiting for the food. Well, standing in the line for the food, that was the most horrible thing to see it. I wasn't accustomed-- you know, [INAUDIBLE] that's why they left us for two days, to, to get accustomed to the life. Staying for the food-- they gave us that soup. It was a vegeta-- vegetable soup, a vegetarian soup-- more water than, than vegetable. I couldn't eat it-- had such a smell. Has-- it was against me. I couldn't eat it. I better, I better starve. I couldn't eat it. But my life was my parents were still home in ghetto. My Moishe, my brother Moishe, was working by the Germans. He had a pass. And he told them that my s-- his sister is in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he in there-- they gave him permission, you know? Why, they liked him as a good worker, to bring me some food in the camp. So that was the most beautiful thing, exciting, when he brought me a package of food. Of course, I shared with all the girls. And when they called me to the gate, and I was so happy to see my brother. I never forgot his face.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted so much to talk to him and tell him what was doing. But they pushed me back. He gave me that little package. And then, and then they let him go. I came into the barrack. They all were so happy with me. Especially I got acquainted with a young girl. Her name was Chanka. She came with her mother who was in the late 30s. And Chanka was 16, a beautiful blonde girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her mother, she, she took a liking to me. And she says, [? Rachelka ?], would you like to be friends with my daughter, Chanka? I says, why not? I'm friends with everybody. So but this didn't take long, of course. Her mother got sick. The typhus was going-- you know was-- a lot of people got struck with typhus in camp-- a lot of people, a lot of young girls and boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And while I was immune to ty-- my immunity, I could visit typh-- you know, sick people, whoever had typhus. You just get it once in your life. So I got acquainted with Chanka. She was a very lovely girl. And we became like two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Chanka's mother got struck with typhus. I wanted to bring her some little food what my brother Moishe brought me-- a little soup, you know? My mother cooked. And she put it in, in, in a, in a little dish. You know, I don't know how-- you know, in a little box, whatever. And, and, and I wanted to bring that over to her. I wanted to bring black coffee to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came in, I couldn't recognize her. I couldn't describe how. It's hard to describe how the ward looked in that typhus ward. Women were laying on one side, men on the other side, naked, with all insects, with all flies flying around their hair and just a piece of paper covered the body. And all of them [INAUDIBLE] to the last breath. When Chanka stood outside, but I went in. But I never told her how her mother looked. I don't want to bring her pain. But the woman who was watching me, I told her that I had typhus in, in ghetto. She gave me the permission to go in. And next day, we went hurry up from work. And I says, Chanka, let's go. I want to visit your mother. Because she was so desperate I should go in and give something for her mother and visit her. Meantime, they took out all these people from that pa-- from that ward and send them-- we had a forest, not a crematorium. In that forest, they used to dig and bury all these people. And whoever was alive, they gave him a bullet here, a bullet there. You know, some of them were buried alive. And who did it is the-- our own inmates from the-- from camp, the men. They were digging these caves and buried these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I came to the barrack, she says to me, the woman, the nurse-- called herself nurse-- she was an inmate like we all. But they picked her up to be a nurse, watching nobody should go into that ward. And she says to me, she's not there. Your mother's not in. She says, where did she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: She looked at her and she says, I'm sorry to tell you, your mother was taking out. The girl went into a shock. She says-- she started to scream. She says, don't scream here. You scream in your barrack but not here. She was mean, the girl-- the girl what was watching that ward.

speaker: interviewer
text: The nurse.

speaker: interviewee
text: The nurse. And I took her on. I tried to talk to her. I told her, Chanka, look, you're not the only one you lost your mother. We all envied her she had came with her mother. You lost her. What can you do? Well, try to, to save yourself. You mother wanted you to live. She, she shook her head. And she says, I don't want to live. And I'm not going to live. Well, day by day, this girl stopped, she stopped living. She started dreaming. She went in like in a trance, like she's not herself. When she got a piece of bread, she looked at that bread. And she didn't even feel like eating it. She didn't want to eat. She punished herself. She didn't washed her face. When going to work, she worked next to my machine. I says, Chanka-- I begged her-- Chanka, try to wash your face. Try to comb your blonde hair, comb your hair. Because blonde hair gets dirty very fast, faster. And dark hair, you don't see the dirt. But blonde hair, somehow, you see like grease on the hair. She looked at me. She didn't answer me. She says-- one time she was so annoyed with me, she says, mind your own business and let me do whatever I want. I just don't want to live. If I don't have my mother, I don't want to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: I says, OK. I'm not going to bother you. But you cannot-- you are like we all here. Why should you be different than we all? She didn't answer me. She looked up with her blue eyes. She stared at me. She didn't cry. She didn't say-- but it was an appeal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every month, we have a selection in the camp. I stood next to Chanka. And we were about a few hundred girls on that sel-- selection every month. So the [? fuhrer ?], his name was [? Batenschlager ?] came out with his, you know, with his whip, with the German shepherd. And he looked in every girl's face. Well, some of the girls, they put a little paper rouge they got it, what they tried to, to make themselves look a little red, to look a little healthier. They were afraid for this monster. And he, he pointed to Chanka. She stood next to me. And she held my hand. She gave like a shiver. She didn't want to go out. She knew. She didn't want to live, but she feared for death, you see. The last minute, she feared. I says, he took her out. They took out about, about 10 of them-- send them to that forest and to be killed. We had a, a big box all winter standing near our barrack. The people were laying there like sardines who died in that ward. They took them to that box. When spring arrived, there came with a truck. And they put that-- it was like a stick with a hook. I watched them to put it. Once I worked in the day-- at night, you know. So I was in the daytime home. And I could-- I watched it, the way they came and took out these dead girls, the women out from that box which was next to my bunk and schlepping them on the truck. And the neck, and an arm, and the stomach-- they put that hook like you, you drag a cat from the street, you know, in a box. That's the way he pulled these people on the truck. Sometimes I stood there emotional. My mind didn't work. And I says, oh, God, oh God. You know, some of the girls, I knew. But the same [? Batenschlager ?] did such evil things in camp. One January-- January 1941-- [? Batenschlager ?] with another one, his partner-- he had, we had [? Killerman. ?] We had [? Batenschlager. ?] And he came to our camp. And he walked into our barrack. And he took out three most beautiful girls on New Years' Eve. One was Chanka Melechman. She was a friend of mine. One was from [INAUDIBLE]-- a girl which I forgot her name. And the, the third one, I don't-- I did, I didn't know her. He took them out. And he drag-- he told them, all the girls, to get off from their bunks. And he choose these three beautiful girls. He took them to his office. He raped them. And he called an inmate to take them to the forest and bury them. He dipped the clothes in their blood, in their own blood. One of them had a mother. And they sent the clothes with the blood to her mother. That was the most horrible thing, the news to hear in camp. There was going on such evil things, from hunger and from killings and from sending away every transport. Every, every move, we lived with fear. From that, it was, it was unbearable. But we strengthened our health. We strengthened our bones.

speaker: interviewer
text: We wanted to live. And we hoped that any day-- America, America, where are you? You're the biggest country in the world. Russia, where are you? England-- you know, where are you, the free countries? Come help us. Don't you know that we are-- that there are concentrations-- people are getting killed? American Jewish people, don't you know? Don't you see? Don't you hear what was going on in the world? But was just talking. That's what we talked among ourselves and dreaming, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, the holiday-- Simchat Torah, you know-- came. Hitler decided to liquidate on September. He arrived to Poland on September. He took September 1st. He finished off all the Jews on September and the two weeks of the holidays, all done September-- the most beautiful weather, the most beautiful time in our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came in in September. That was Simchat Torah. Was a holiday-- Yom Kippur, Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashana. We didn't know. We knew. I ate one slice of bread, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked my own questions. When I remember-- when I was a little girl, so many people fainted with all the good foods on Yom Kippur. They couldn't stand a day to fast. Here with one slice of bread, you know, I ate the night before, and I fasted for so many hours. It didn't bother me. It bothered me. But somehow, I survived. And I didn't faint. And I worked the whole day. I just envy my own strength. I-- on Simchat Torah day, my meister walked over to me. And he says, Miss Warszauer, Skarzysko was evacuated. 11 o'clock, 11 o'clock, 11 o'clock they came in to our [INAUDIBLE], 11 o'clock on Simchat Torah, [INAUDIBLE] Skarzysko was evacuated again. These numbers, these dates, always, you now, the same time. I cried. I didn't want to show him that I'm crying. I, I felt that I have temperature. I felt my head is coming off. I felt like the same feeling like I had typhus. My whole body shook up. I'm walking. We walk. We walk in five in a row. We walk, you know, few hundred girls walking from the fac-- from the work, go back to the, to the camp. While I walk in, I says, now, it's my last chance. I don't have anymore food sending from my home. I lost my parents. And perhaps [? Moishele ?] and Shmulek is gone with my parents. I am going to be alone in the camp. I have no money like parents gave them to trade with the Poles to bring me a piece of bread or two. I have no possibility to survive. And I says, I didn't think about surviving anymore, tell you the truth. I'm walking. I'm walking with the girls. I'm thinking. I'm thinking. I'm thinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of a sudden, like from heaven, I'm walking through the gate. About 20 people, men and women, sitting in a corner, with full of dust, scratched up, dirty, dirty, and I-- dirty. I says, where are they? But the faces looked still fresh, not worn out from a concentration, you know? Not skinny-- some of them didn't look skinny. Because they-- you know, some of them, they had enough food in ghetto. Some of them were starving. Hey, you know, like was always complicated. Some had, some not, you know? And like from heaven, I see, a hand stretches out. [? Rachelka ?]. Rachelka. Who could it be? I knew my sister had no chance. She lived in [INAUDIBLE]. She had a husband with two children. She probably was evacuated from her city. Esther was evacuated on Yom Kippur. Who could I expect? A friend? I didn't pay attention. I keep walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I will, I will see her later. Because you couldn't walk out from your row. You had to stay on appell to be counted. My sister got up from the group. And she comes with her hands like that, like a, like a-- flying to me with her hands. And like an angel, she fly-- She was fly. She says, she didn't walk. She says, [? Rachela ?], [? Rachela ?], my [NON-ENGLISH] And I looked at her. And I says, you-- ah! I says. She says, don't cry. She says. She put her finger and made sure that I'm not going to cry. When you put your finger in your teeth, you cannot cry. Because you are-- you stuck it. You stuck you, your teeth. And I can't wait for the row, for the appell to be over. But it takes so long. It sometimes took an hour, sometimes a half an hour, sometimes 20 minutes, wherever he was in a mood, [? Batenschlager, ?] the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your-- I just want to make sure that you say which sister this was.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is sister, sister, Tobcha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tobcha.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tobcha. The appell is over. We run to each other's arms. I says, what? I'm so-- where? She says, don't cry. I didn't ask her about her husband. I didn't ask her about her children, Tobcha's children. She says, Shmulek is here, too. Shmulek is here, too. Where is he? But they were taking him to [? Warsaw. ?] Oh, my God. He wouldn't have a chance to survive, because [? Warsaw ?] is the worst camp from all three. She says, maybe he will get out from there. Maybe we will, we will be able to do something with protection. If you had some protection, you know, sometimes you could do it, you know, to take somebody out and to another-- you know, to work out to be together. Well, they, they gave her the number. And of course, I spoke to the Yiddish, to the Jewish police. And I told her, that's my sister. And he says, you're going to be together. And we slept on the same together in one [INAUDIBLE] together. And [INAUDIBLE] is one bed, you know, together, on top. Watching my sister twisting and twisting and twisting, and not sleeping, and the whole, the whole bunk was moving. I realized what she went through. But I didn't want to ask her right away. I, I knew she's going to tell me her pain herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, next day, we came back from work. She was assigned to work the same place where I worked, but a little different ammunition things. But we were together. Now, I says-- now, she says, my dear sister, would you like to know my life story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't say nothing. She says, yes I tell you. I tell you my [INAUDIBLE]. I tell you my life. I started to cry and cry and cry when she told me that when they evacuated [INAUDIBLE], she with her husband, and with the two little girls were trying to build a bunker in their own house, underneath in the kitchen. And they were thinking maybe they will survive after the evacuation, maybe [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: But how long can you sit in a bunker like that? They sat for two week. And they decided, after two weeks, to go out and to seek for some foods, to ask, to go to, to a, to a, to these people, Polish, the Polish people, with whom they left all their goods. Because they owned textile, you know, business. They left a lot of goods with them before the evacuation. So they said, maybe they can give us some bread, some water, some whatever. So [INAUDIBLE] her husband says, listen, Tobcha, my dear, I will walk out with Rachel hand by hand first. You will stay with Chajale. Hold on your arm behind the gate. If you hear something, some flick, [INAUDIBLE] goes on, if you hear some noise, go back to the bunker. Perhaps you will have a better chance. And soon when [INAUDIBLE] walks out with Rachel, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this German [INAUDIBLE] was standing right on the corner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, halt. That's all what Tobcha heard and a loud noise, the bullet. Because she was unaware. He fell on the ground-- she didn't see him fell-- with her, with Rachel. She picked herself up and walked back and kept the wishes which [INAUDIBLE] left. He told her to go back and give herself a chance. And she went back to the bunker, of course crying and crying-- this I don't have to tell you-- and thinking perhaps she will take her own life and her Chajale's life, her second child. But she couldn't do it herself. So she came to, to a conclusion, that she will put, give herself over to the police. She will walk over in the morning. She will come out from the bunker. She will take Chajale, the younger one. And she will give herself over to the police. Let them do whatever they want to decide. Meantime, thinking to herself, she heard meowing, like a cat. Meow. Rachela, the bullet didn't hit her. It just went and injured her head. But she was alive. Laying next to her father, she touched him. She realized he's dead. She left him. And a girl at the age of seven remembered to go back in that bunker and shoveling that floor and wanted to find her mother, and meowing, ma. Meow. My sister thought there's a cat. But the girl was meowing, ma, ma. But she hadn't got the strength to meow. But to my sister, it sta-- it sounded like a cat. When she opened the opening, the door, she did see it's her child, Rachel. She took her down. Oh, God. She examines her head. It was injured. Blood was coming out. She didn't have no water to put on her child's head. She tear a sheet and took a piece and wet it with her own urine and put it to her child's head. In the morning--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the morning, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, she, she took her two children and came out from her hiding place, which I wrote a poem. I gave you to read that poem about my two nieces in their hiding nest. And she gave her over to the police. Let them do whatever they want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, she wasn't the only one. They were already about around, around about 10, 15 Jewish people, who tried to do the same trick like they did. But it wasn't successful. They find them. And because of hunger, they have to give themselves up. How long can they sit in a bunker with no, no water and no-- not a little crumb of food? So they send them Skarzysko. In Skarzyko, next day, Simchat Torah, she came with her children. My parents didn't ask her about her husband. She made it with her hand, not questions. She didn't want to tell her story.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came in with her two children to my parents' home. On Simchat Torah day, on a Sunday, arouse 11 o'clock. [INAUDIBLE] They took the Jews away. OK. We're going to stop there.
